findings.message: session.commit() is called inside the loop, so every item is persisted in its own independent transaction. If the process fails (exception, crash, connectivity loss) after the k-th commit, the first k items are durably written and cannot be rolled back — the caller has no mechanism to recover or undo the partial batch. The function's public contract gives no indication that it is non-atomic.
findings.suggestion: Remove commit() from the loop. Call session.add() for every item first, then session.commit() once after the loop, then do a single-pass refresh. If the caller manages the transaction externally, omit the commit entirely and let the caller decide when to finalise.

findings.message: session.refresh() is called immediately after each per-loop commit. If refresh raises mid-batch (e.g. transient DB error, connection drop), the session is left in an indeterminate state: some items are already durably committed in closed transactions, the current item's add() may be in-flight, and the caller cannot safely roll back or retry. The combination of commit-then-refresh inside the loop creates two independent failure points per iteration, each of which can strand a partial write.
findings.suggestion: Defer all refresh() calls to a second pass after the single post-loop commit. Also note that expire_on_commit=False is already set in build_session_factory, so the refresh() calls are unnecessary — objects retain their populated state across commit without a round-trip.

findings.message: The names parameter is entirely unbounded. Unlike list_items, which enforces MAX_PAGE_SIZE on reads, this write path has no cap. A caller can pass a list of arbitrary length, producing N individual commits (under the current implementation) or one very large transaction (after the fix), either of which can cause prolonged lock contention, excessive memory pressure, or a single enormous all-or-nothing failure with no partial-progress option.
findings.suggestion: Add a MAX_BATCH_SIZE constant (analogous to MAX_PAGE_SIZE) and either raise ValueError for oversized inputs or chunk the work into bounded sub-batches, each committed atomically.
